perturbation.original_text: SELLER agrees to comply with all applicable Federal, State and local laws, executive orders, rules and regulations applicable to its performance under this Agreement. SELLER agrees to comply with the following Federal Acquisition Regulation clauses, which shall be deemed incorporated by reference:
perturbation.changed_text: SELLER agrees to comply with some Federal, State and local laws applicable to its performance under this Agreement. Compliance with other laws is at SELLER's discretion. SELLER agrees to comply with some Federal Acquisition Regulation clauses, which may be deemed incorporated by reference:
perturbation.explanation: By weakening the commitment to comply with "all applicable" laws to "some" laws, and making the compliance with FAR clauses discretionary, the contract now introduces a legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the Fair Labor Standards Act, which mandates minimum wage requirements for employees. By stating compliance is discretionary, the SELLER could potentially violate minimum wage laws without explicitly breaching the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 29 - LABOR 29 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 29 - LABOR CHAPTER 8 - FAIR LABOR STANDARDS Sec. 206 - Minimum wage From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §206. Minimum wage (a) Employees engaged in commerce; home workers in Puerto Rico and Virgin Islands; employees in American Samoa; seamen on American vessels; agricultural employees Every employer shall pay to each of his employees who in any workweek is engaged in commerce or in the production of goods for commerce, or is employed in an enterprise engaged in commerce or in the production of goods for commerce, wages at the following rates: (1) except as otherwise provided in this section, not less than— (A) $5.85 an hour, beginning on the 60th day after May 25, 2007; (B) $6.55 an hour, beginning 12 months after that 60th day; and (C) $7.25 an hour, beginning 24 months after that 60th day; (2) if such employee is a home worker in Puerto Rico or the Virgin Islands, not less than the minimum piece rate prescribed by regulation or order; or, if no such minimum piece rate is in effect, any piece rate adopted by such employer which shall yield, to the proportion or class of employees prescribed by regulation or order, not less than the applicable minimum hourly wage rate. Such minimum piece rates or employer piece rates shall be commensurate with, and shall be paid in lieu of, the minimum hourly wage rate applicable under the provisions of this section. The Administrator, or his authorized representative, shall have power to make such regulations or orders as are necessary or appropriate to carry out any of the provisions of this paragraph, including the power without limiting the generality of the foregoing, to define any operation or occupation which is performed by such home work employees in Puerto Rico or the Virgin Islands; to establish minimum piece rates for any operation or occupation so defined; to prescribe the method and procedure for ascertaining and promulgating minimum piece rates; to prescribe standards for employer piece rates, including the proportion or class of employees who shall
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's commitment to comply with 'all applicable' laws, now stating compliance with only 'some' laws is required, and making compliance with FAR clauses discretionary. This directly contradicts the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), which mandates minimum wage requirements for employees as stated in the provided snippet, 'Every employer shall pay to each of his employees who in any workweek is engaged in commerce...wages at the following rates'. By making compliance discretionary, the SELLER could potentially violate minimum wage laws without explicitly breaching the modified contract, undermining the mandatory nature of the FLSA.

perturbation.original_text: H.4 INSURANCE The Subcontractor shall secure, pay the premiums for and keep in force until the expiration of the 15 <PAGE> resulting Subcontract, and any renewal thereof, adequate insurance as provided below, such insurance to specifically include liability assumed by the Subcontractor under this contract. a. Workman's Compensation insurance as required by law of the State. b. Comprehensive bodily injury liability insurance with limits of not less than $500,000 for each accident c. Property damage liability with a limit of not less than $100,000 for each accident. d. Automotive bodily injury liability insurance with limits of not less than $200,000 for each person and $500,000 for each accident, and property damage liability insurance, with a limit of not less than $40,000 for each accident. Each policy of insurance shall contain an endorsement that any cancellation or material change in the coverage adversely affecting the Government's interest shall not be effective unless the insurer or the Subcontractor gives written notice of cancellation or change, as required by the Subcontract Administrator. When the coverage is provided by self-insurance, the Subcontractor shall not change or decrease the coverage without the Subcontract Administrator's prior approval. A certificate of each policy of insurance shall be furnished to the Subcontract Administrator within ten (10) days after notice of award certifying, among other things, that the policy contains the aforementioned endorsement. The insurance company providing the above insurance shall be satisfactory to the Government. Notice of policy changes shall be furnished to the Subcontract Administrator. The substance of this clause shall be made to flow down to any lower tier subcontractors.
perturbation.changed_text: H.4 INSURANCE The Subcontractor shall secure insurance, until the expiration of the 15 <PAGE> resulting Subcontract, and any renewal thereof, to include liability assumed by the Subcontractor under this contract. a. Workman's Compensation insurance. b. Bodily injury liability insurance. c. Property damage liability insurance. d. Automotive bodily injury liability insurance and property damage liability insurance. Notice of policy changes shall be furnished to the Subcontract Administrator. The substance of this clause shall be made to flow down to any lower tier subcontractors.
perturbation.explanation: By removing specific limits and some of the requirements the contract is non-compliant.
perturbation.law_explanation: By only requiring 'Workman's Compensation insurance' without explicitly stating 'as required by law of the State,' and removing specific liability limits, the modified text now contradicts the Virginia Workers' Compensation Act. This Act mandates that employers in Virginia must carry worker's compensation insurance, and the omission removes that guarantee.

Definitions and General Provisions 6/23/2025 § 65.2-101 . Definitions. As used in this title: "Average weekly wage" means: 1. a. The earnings of the injured employee in the employment in which he was working at the time of the injury during the period of 52 weeks immediately preceding the date of the injury, divided by 52; but if the injured employee lost more than seven consecutive calendar days during such period, although not in the same week, then the earnings for the remainder of the 52 weeks shall be divided by the number of weeks remaining after the time so lost has been deducted. When the employment prior to the injury extended over a period of less than 52 weeks, the method of dividing the earnings during that period by the number of weeks and parts thereof during which the employee earned wages shall be followed, provided that results fair and just to both parties will be thereby obtained. When, by reason of a shortness of time during which the employee has been in the employment of his employer or the casual nature or terms of his employment, it is impractical to compute the average weekly wages as above defined, regard shall be had to the average weekly amount which during the 52 weeks previous to the injury was being earned by a person of t
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits specific liability limits and requirements for insurance coverage, which contradicts the original contract's intention to ensure adequate insurance. The Law Explanation suggests this omission conflicts with the Virginia Workers' Compensation Act, potentially removing a required guarantee. Although only one scraped law snippet is provided, it highlights the importance of definitions within the context of workers' compensation law, implying that the omission of 'as required by law of the State' and specific limits weakens the enforceable obligation. The original text ensures compliance with specific legal standards, while the modified text does not, creating a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 21. INDEMNIFICATION The employees of SELLER and the BUYER engaged in performance under this Agreement shall at all times be deemed to be performing as independent contractors and not as agents or employees of the other and the acts and omission of such employees shall be deemed to be those of their respective employers. SELLER shall indemnify and hold harmless the BUYER and its employees from and against any and all losses, claims, demands, judgments, costs, and expenses, of every nature and kind, arising out of or incidental to, or in any way resulting from the acts or omission of SELLER or SELLER's employees while acting within the scope of their employment.
perturbation.changed_text: 21. INDEMNIFICATION The employees of SELLER and the BUYER engaged in performance under this Agreement shall at all times be deemed to be performing as independent contractors and not as agents or employees of the other and the acts and omission of such employees shall be deemed to be those of their respective employers. SELLER shall indemnify and hold harmless the BUYER from and against any losses, claims, demands, judgments, costs, and expenses, arising out of the acts or omission of SELLER or SELLER's employees.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'of every nature and kind' and 'while acting within the scope of their employment,' the indemnification clause becomes overly broad and potentially unenforceable, creating legal ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could potentially violate the Virginia Consumer Protection Act if the indemnification clause is interpreted to cover losses arising from SELLER's actions outside the scope of their employment or from BUYER's own negligence, thereby shifting undue burden onto the SELLER.

Virginia Consumer Protection Act Read Chapter § 59.1-196 Title § 59.1-197 Intent § 59.1-198 Definitions § 59.1-199 Exclusions § 59.1-200 (Effective until July 1, 2025) Prohibited practices § 59.1-200 (Effective July 1, 2025) Prohibited practices § 59.1-200.1 Prohibited practices; foreclosure rescue § 59.1-201 Civil investigative orders § 59.1-201.1 Attorney General empowered to issue civil investigative demands § 59.1-202 Assurances of voluntary compliance § 59.1-203 Restraining prohibited acts § 59.1-204 Individual action for damages or penalty § 59.1-204.1 Tolling of limitation § 59.1-205 Additional relief § 59.1-206 Civil penalties; attorney fees § 59.1-207 Unintentional violations Virginia Law Library The Code of Virginia, Constitution of Virginia, Charters, Authorities, Compacts and Uncodified Acts are now available in EPub eBook format. Helpful Resources Virginia Code Commission Virginia Register of Regulations U.S. Constitution For Developers The Virginia Law website data is available via a web service.
perturbation.justification: The modification removes 'of every nature and kind' and 'while acting within the scope of their employment' from the indemnification clause. The Law Explanation suggests this creates an overly broad indemnification clause, potentially violating the Virginia Consumer Protection Act (VCPA) if it covers losses from actions outside the SELLER's employment scope or from the BUYER's negligence. The original text limited the scope of indemnification to acts or omissions 'while acting within the scope of their employment' and covered losses 'of every nature and kind' within that scope, while the modified text broadens it. Since this change potentially shifts undue burden onto the SELLER, as stated in the Law Explanation, and the VCPA aims to protect consumers from unfair practices, it creates a contradiction, especially given that the Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Virginia Consumer Protection Act directly, which supports the explanation that the modified text creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 17. WARRANTY SELLER warrants and implies that the items delivered hereunder are merchantable and fit for use for the particular purpose described in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 17. WARRANTY SELLER warrants that the items delivered hereunder are as described.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose significantly reduces the protection for the buyer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the warranty of merchantability, the agreement contradicts the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC § 2-314), which implies a warranty that goods are merchantable (fit for their ordinary purpose) if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. This omission deprives the BUYER of a standard protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: , from early projects like the restatement of basic legal subjects to a collaboration with NCCUSL in developing and monitoring the Uniform Commercial Code. View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. View more information about the NBA collections Explore the NBA Oral History collections Biddle Collections The Collections consist of manuscript collections, rare books, oral histories, and a select number of Penn Carey Law School’s records. The manuscript collections include the papers of George Wharton Pepper, Bernie Segal, William Draper Lewis, and David L. Bazelon. The Raymond F. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. Some records relating to the operations of Penn Carey Law School are maintained by the University Archives . Please contact us for more information. View more information about the Biddle Law Library Collections Explore the collection of The Report the Law School’s yearbook Explore the Penn Carey Law Oral History Project collection Contact & Hours The Archives and Special Collections office is located on the fourth floor of Tanenbaum Hall. The mailing address is: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School Biddle Law Library - Archives Department 3501 Sansom Street Philadelphia, PA 19104-3406 For questions about the Archives, the Rare Books Collection, or other special collections, please email the Archives Department at biddlearchives@law.upenn.edu . The Archives is open Tuesday-Friday, 10AM to 4PM. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of the visit. Visitors will be asked to show identification. Policies Registration All researchers are asked to fill out our Researcher Registration Form. This form can be filled out prior to your visit or on the day of your arrival. Archives - Researcher Registration Form On-site Research Items in the Archives do not circulate. If you wish to come to the Archives to conduct research, you may do so free of charge. However, you must contact the department ahead of time to schedule an appointment. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of desired research date. Visitors to the Law School will be asked to show identification. Remote Research For patrons who cannot travel to the Law School to conduct research, the Archives will assist in the identification of potentially relevant materials for your research topic or for photocopying. We do not transcribe, paraphrase, or otherwise interpret information for the researcher. Research requests generally take 2-3 weeks to complete, depending on the nature of the question and the number of outstanding requests at the time of submission. In addition, the Archives can only offer one hour of research assistance, patrons are asked to make an appointment for requests that will take longer than one hour to complete. Please also note that remote research requests are limited, and the Archives can only handle copying/scanning requests up to 20 individual documents. If a request exceeds this number, we suggest you schedule an appointment to visit the Archives in person. Handling Materials Wash hands before touching materials. Protective gloves may be required. Remove only one folder from a box at a time. Documents should be maintained in the exact order they are arranged in each file. If a document is out of order, notify the Archives staff. Make no marks or erasures on any document. Use strips provided to mark pages for copying. Eating or drinking is not permitted. Use pencils only, no pens or markers. Laptop
perturbation.justification: The original text includes an implied warranty of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. The changed text removes these warranties, which contradicts UCC § 2-314, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind." By omitting the warranty of merchantability, the buyer loses a standard protection afforded under the UCC, creating a legal contradiction.
